subject: english
topic_title: Nnamdi Azikiwe, Relative Pronouns, Building Vocabulary, and Narrative Writing
learning_objectives: Summarize the key ideas in Nnamdi Azikiwe.
Apply Relative Pronouns correctly in sentences.
Use vocabulary related to Building correctly in context.
Write a clear Narrative using the expected features and structure.

sections.heading: Summary: Nnamdi Azikiwe
sections.content: The passage focuses on Nnamdi Azikiwe Sport Complex, the ultra- modern stadium in Enugu. The complex is said to have been named after the man who has given Nigerian sportsmen the greatest inspiration through his involvement in sports.

sections.heading: Structure: Relative Pronouns
sections.content: A relative pronoun is a word that relates or links an adjectival or relative clause to the word it describes (i.e. its antecedent. They include pronouns such as who, whom, that, which, whose, where, etc. Note: the pronouns who (subject), whom (object) and whose (possessive) are used strictly in reference to people. The word ‘which’ is used for things, while ‘that’ goes with people or things.

sections.heading: Vocabulary Development: Building
sections.content: Trench – A long narrow ditch or hole dug in the ground. Concrete – Mixture of cement, sand, gravel and water that hardens as it dries. Foundation – Part of a house on which the other parts test for support. D.P.C (Damp Proof Course): Layer of material application to prevent moisture or damp from passing through a wall or floor. Scaffold: A structure made of scaffolding, for workers to stand on while working on a building. Glazier: A person whose job is to put glass in windows. German floor: Concrete floor made over the short walls of a foundation to ground floor. Plumbers: An individual who fits water pipes, basins, baths, watertanks etc. Gravel: Pebbles and pieces of rock coarse than sand.

sections.heading: Writing – Narrative
sections.content: A narrative essay is one in which the writer tells or relates a story or a past event. It involves giving an account of an incident or event in which somebody was involved to an audience that has not experienced it. Every narrative involves: people, action, time and; place. Features: Tense forms (use the past tense forms). Natural sequences of events. Linkage of events: let there be unity and coherence. Arrangement into different paragraphs – introduction, body and conclusion.
